exercises.title: Positional Arguments
exercises.description: Positional arguments must be passed in the exact order the parameters are defined. Call the function with arguments in the correct order.
exercises.starterCode:
def introduce(first_name, last_name, age):
    """Print a personalized introduction."""
    print(f"Hi, I'm {first_name} {last_name}, {age} years old.")

# TODO: Call introduce with 'Alice', 'Johnson', 28 (positional)
introduce("Alice", "Johnson", 28)

# TODO: Call introduce with 'Bob', 'Smith', 35
introduce("Bob", "Smith", 35)

# TODO: Call introduce using keyword arguments (any order)
introduce(age=22, last_name="Garcia", first_name="Carlos")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Hi, I'm Alice Johnson, 28 years old.
Hi, I'm Bob Smith, 35 years old.
Hi, I'm Carlos Garcia, 22 years old.
hints: Positional arguments are matched by position, left to right.
Keyword arguments can be passed in any order: introduce(age=22, last_name='...')

exercises.title: Multiple Parameters and Type Hints
exercises.description: Type hints document what types parameters expect. Define a function with type hints and test it.
exercises.starterCode:
def format_price(amount: float, currency: str, decimals: int) -> str:
    """Format a price with currency symbol and decimal places."""
    return f"{currency}{amount:.{decimals}f}"

# Test the function
print(format_price(29.99, "$", 2))
print(format_price(1234.5, "€", 2))
print(format_price(100, "¥", 0))
print(format_price(3.14159, "£", 4))
hints: :.{decimals}f in an f-string uses the variable as the precision.
Type hints (: float, : str, -> str) are documentation — Python doesn't enforce them.

exercises.title: Arguments vs Parameters
exercises.description: Parameters are the names in the function definition; arguments are the values passed when calling. Demonstrate by tracing how values flow through function calls.
exercises.starterCode:
def power_info(base: int, exponent: int) -> None:
    """Print information about base raised to exponent."""
    result = base ** exponent
    print(f"{base}^{exponent} = {result}")

# Each call passes different arguments to the same parameters
power_info(2, 10)
power_info(3, 4)
power_info(10, 6)

# Pass variables as arguments
my_base = 5
my_exp = 3
power_info(my_base, my_exp)
hints: The function parameters (base, exponent) receive the argument values each time.
You can pass variables, literals, or expressions as arguments.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Parameters vs Arguments
warmup.description: Parameters are defined in def; arguments are passed when calling.
warmup.starterCode:
def add(a, b):    # a and b are PARAMETERS
    return a + b

result = add(3, 7)  # 3 and 7 are ARGUMENTS
print(result)

# Keyword arguments
print(add(b=10, a=5))  # order doesn't matter with keywords
